Title: Company Newsletter Draft
ContextText: Monthly internal newsletter about upcoming team events and office updates.
Explanation: Internal communications containing no personal data are typically classified as Internal.

Title: Public Press Release
ContextText: Official press release about new product launch, approved for distribution.
Explanation: Documents explicitly approved for public distribution should be classified as Public.

Title: Employee Salary Spreadsheet
ContextText: Spreadsheet containing names, positions, and annual salaries for all department staff.
Explanation: Salary data combined with employee names is highly sensitive PII requiring the strictest classification.

Title: Product Roadmap Q3
ContextText: Strategic product roadmap with competitive analysis and unreleased feature plans.
Explanation: Strategic business documents with competitive intelligence are Confidential — disclosure could harm the company.

Title: Open Source License File
ContextText: Standard MIT license text for an open-source library used in the project.
Explanation: Open-source licenses are inherently public documents.

Title: Vendor NDA Agreement
ContextText: Non-disclosure agreement template with vendor contact details and terms.
Explanation: NDAs contain business-sensitive terms and should be classified as Confidential.

Title: Office Floor Plan
ContextText: Building layout showing office seating, exits, and common areas.
Explanation: Office layouts are internal operational documents — not public but not highly sensitive.

Title: Customer Health Records
ContextText: Medical screening results for insurance applicants including diagnoses and medications.
Explanation: Health records are special category data under GDPR Article 9 and require the highest protection.

Title: Database Backup Script
ContextText: Automated backup script containing database connection strings and credentials.
Explanation: Scripts with embedded credentials must be Highly Restricted — leaked credentials can lead to data breaches.

Title: Marketing Campaign Materials
ContextText: Draft social media posts and banner ads for upcoming campaign.
Explanation: Pre-release marketing materials are Internal until approved for publication.

Title: Board Meeting Minutes
ContextText: Minutes from quarterly board meeting discussing financial performance and M&A targets.
Explanation: Board minutes with M&A discussions are material non-public information — Highly Restricted.

Title: Student Enrollment Records
ContextText: List of enrolled students with grades, addresses, and parent contact information.
Explanation: Student records are protected under FERPA and must be Highly Restricted.

Title: API Documentation
ContextText: Public API reference docs for third-party developer integration.
Explanation: Documentation intended for external developers is public by design.

Title: Employee Performance Review
ContextText: Annual performance evaluation with manager comments and improvement plans.
Explanation: Performance reviews contain personal opinions and career-impacting data — Confidential.

Title: Incident Response Playbook
ContextText: Internal security procedures for handling data breaches and cyber incidents.
Explanation: Security playbooks reveal defensive capabilities — classifying them protects your response strategy.

Title: Customer SSN Export
ContextText: CSV export containing customer names and Social Security Numbers for tax reporting.
Explanation: SSNs are the most sensitive PII — always Highly Restricted with encryption required.

Title: Anonymized Survey Results
ContextText: Aggregated employee satisfaction survey with no identifying information.
Explanation: Properly anonymized data has lower classification, but keep Internal since it reveals organizational sentiment.

Title: Pseudonymized Research Data
ContextText: Clinical trial data with patient IDs replaced by random codes. Key file stored separately.
Explanation: Pseudonymized data is still personal data under GDPR if re-identification is possible — Confidential minimum.

Title: GDPR Subject Access Request
ContextText: Compiled response to a data subject access request including all personal data held.
Explanation: SAR responses consolidate all personal data about an individual — they require maximum protection.

Title: Company Holiday Schedule
ContextText: Official company-wide holiday and office closure calendar for the year.
Explanation: While seemingly simple, holiday schedules posted externally are Public. The trick is recognizing not everything from email is sensitive.

Title: Unencrypted USB Found
ContextText: A USB drive labeled 'Client Data' is found in the parking lot. It contains unencrypted spreadsheets.
Explanation: Unknown USB devices should never be plugged into workstations. IT Security can safely analyze them in an isolated environment.
OptionA: Plug it into your workstation to check contents
OptionB: Hand it to IT Security for forensic analysis
OptionC: Place it in lost and found
OptionD: Format the drive and reuse it

Title: Email Forwarding Request
ContextText: A colleague asks you to forward a confidential contract to their personal Gmail for 'weekend review'.
Explanation: Confidential data should never leave corporate systems. VPN access allows secure remote work without data exfiltration risk.
OptionA: Forward it — they need to work
OptionB: Decline and suggest using the company VPN instead
OptionC: Forward it but password-protect the attachment
OptionD: Report the request to your manager

Title: Printer Left Unattended
ContextText: You notice confidential HR documents sitting in the shared printer tray. No one is nearby.
Explanation: Collect abandoned confidential printouts and return to the owning department. Don't read contents unnecessarily or destroy others' documents.
OptionA: Leave them — someone will collect them
OptionB: Read them to identify the owner and deliver personally
OptionC: Collect them and bring to HR directly

Title: Cloud Storage Sync
ContextText: You discover that a team member has been syncing the entire 'Confidential' project folder to their personal Dropbox.
Explanation: Unauthorized data transfers to personal cloud storage is a security incident that must be reported to IT Security for proper remediation.
OptionA: Ask them to stop and delete the synced copy
OptionB: Report to IT Security immediately
OptionC: Ignore it — many people do this
OptionD: Help them set up the approved enterprise cloud storage instead

Title: Expired Access Rights
ContextText: A former contractor still has active access to the customer database. They left 3 months ago.
Explanation: Revoke access immediately and audit logs for unauthorized activity. Stale accounts are a top attack vector.
OptionA: Leave it — they might come back
OptionB: Revoke access and audit recent activity logs
OptionC: Send the contractor an email asking them not to use it

Title: Backup Tape Disposal
ContextText: Old backup tapes from 2019 need to be disposed of. They contain customer PII.
Explanation: Media containing PII must be securely destroyed with documented chain of custody. Simple overwriting may not be sufficient for tapes.
OptionA: Throw them in the regular trash
OptionB: Degauss and physically destroy, then document the destruction
OptionC: Store them in a closet indefinitely

Title: Vendor Data Sharing
ContextText: A vendor requests raw customer analytics data to 'improve their integration'. No DPA exists.
Explanation: Never share personal data with third parties without a Data Processing Agreement. This is a legal requirement under GDPR Article 28.
OptionA: Share the data — they're a trusted vendor
OptionB: Share anonymized/aggregated data only
OptionC: Refuse until a Data Processing Agreement is signed
OptionD: Share a sample of 100 records as a compromise

Title: Database Migration
ContextText: During a database migration, you need to transfer customer records including EU citizen data to a US-based server.
Explanation: Cross-border transfers of EU personal data require appropriate safeguards like SCCs under GDPR Chapter V.
OptionB: Ensure Standard Contractual Clauses are in place first
OptionC: Only transfer non-EU customer data
OptionD: Encrypt the data and proceed without legal review

Title: Data Breach Notification
ContextText: You discover that 500 customer email addresses were accidentally exposed in a public API response for 48 hours.
Explanation: Under GDPR Article 33, breaches must be reported to the supervisory authority within 72 hours. Individual notification depends on risk assessment.
OptionA: Fix the API and move on
OptionB: Notify the DPA within 72 hours and assess individual notification needs
OptionC: Wait to see if anyone noticed

Title: Right to Erasure Request
ContextText: A customer requests deletion of all their data, but you have a legal obligation to retain financial records for 7 years.
Explanation: The right to erasure is not absolute. Legal obligations can override it, but you must delete everything not covered by an exemption.
OptionA: Delete everything — customer rights come first
OptionB: Delete what you can, retain legally required records with documented justification
OptionC: Refuse the entire request citing legal obligation
OptionD: Anonymize all records instead of deleting

Title: Screen Sharing in Meeting
ContextText: During a video call with external partners, you need to share your screen. Your desktop has confidential documents open.
Explanation: Always share specific application windows, not full desktop. Close or minimize sensitive content before any screen sharing.
OptionB: Close all confidential documents, then share only the relevant application window
OptionC: Share desktop but minimize sensitive windows

Title: Data Retention Policy
ContextText: Your team has been storing customer support tickets indefinitely. Many contain personal details.
Explanation: Data minimization requires retention limits proportional to the purpose. Anonymization preserves analytical value while meeting privacy obligations.
OptionA: Keep everything — it might be useful
OptionB: Implement a retention policy: anonymize after 2 years, delete after 5
OptionC: Delete all tickets older than 1 month
OptionD: Move old tickets to a cheaper storage tier

Title: Employee Monitoring Disclosure
ContextText: IT wants to deploy keystroke logging on all employee machines without telling anyone.
Explanation: Employee monitoring requires transparency, a legitimate purpose, and proportionality. Covert monitoring is only justified in exceptional circumstances.
OptionB: Require transparent disclosure to employees and a legitimate purpose assessment first
OptionC: Only monitor executives
OptionD: Deploy it but tell HR only

Title: Password on Sticky Note
ContextText: You notice a colleague has their system password written on a sticky note attached to their monitor.
Explanation: Address security hygiene issues directly and constructively. Suggest alternatives like password managers rather than just criticizing.
OptionB: Privately tell the colleague and suggest a password manager
OptionC: Report them to HR immediately

Title: AI Training Data
ContextText: The data science team wants to use production customer data to train a machine learning model.
Explanation: Using personal data for AI training requires a DPIA, legitimate basis, and ideally synthetic/anonymized data to minimize privacy risk.
OptionA: Allow it — it improves the product
OptionB: Require use of synthetic or properly anonymized data with a DPIA
OptionC: Use only 10% of the data as a compromise
OptionD: Proceed if customers agreed to the ToS

Title: Under GDPR, what is the maximum time allowed to report a data breach to the supervisory authority?
ContextText: Your company just discovered an unauthorized access to customer records.
Explanation: GDPR Article 33 requires breach notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware.
OptionB: 72 hours
OptionC: 7 days

Title: Which GDPR principle requires that personal data be kept no longer than necessary?
ContextText: You are reviewing your company's data retention policies.
Explanation: Storage limitation (Article 5(1)(e)) mandates that data is kept in identifiable form only as long as necessary for the processing purpose.
OptionB: Storage limitation
OptionC: Data minimization

Title: What legal basis does NOT apply under GDPR?
ContextText: Your legal team is evaluating how to justify processing customer data.
Explanation: GDPR Article 6 lists six legal bases. 'Business convenience' is not one — each basis requires specific justification.
OptionB: Legitimate interest
OptionC: Business convenience

Title: When is a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) required under GDPR?
ContextText: Your team plans to implement facial recognition for building access.
Explanation: GDPR Article 35 requires DPIAs when processing is likely to result in high risk, especially with new technologies, profiling, or large-scale processing of sensitive data.
OptionB: When processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals
OptionC: Only when processing children's data

Title: Under GDPR, what right allows individuals to obtain their data in a machine-readable format?
ContextText: A customer wants to transfer their account data to a competitor's service.
Explanation: Data portability (Article 20) enables individuals to receive their data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format.
OptionB: Right to data portability
OptionC: Right to access

Title: A 15-year-old signs up for your social media service. Under GDPR, whose consent do you need?
ContextText: Your platform is expanding to EU markets and needs to handle minor registrations.
Explanation: GDPR Article 8 requires parental consent for children under 16 (or lower age set by member states, minimum 13) for information society services.
OptionB: Parental consent required
OptionC: No consent needed for public platforms

Title: Your company's EU representative resigned. You're a US company with EU customers. What must you do?
ContextText: Legal compliance review reveals the representative gap.
Explanation: Non-EU controllers must designate an EU representative (Article 27). Operating without one is a compliance violation.
OptionB: Appoint a new EU representative under Article 27
OptionC: Stop processing EU data until appointed
OptionD: Notify the lead supervisory authority only

Title: Under CCPA, what must a business provide when a consumer requests to know what personal information is collected?
ContextText: Your California customer service team received a CCPA request.
Explanation: CCPA Section 1798.110 gives consumers the right to request both categories and specific pieces of personal information a business has collected.
OptionA: Deny the request citing trade secrets
OptionB: Provide the categories and specific pieces of personal information collected
OptionC: Direct them to the privacy policy only

Title: Can a business charge different prices to consumers who exercise their CCPA rights?
ContextText: Marketing wants to offer discounts only to customers who allow data selling.
Explanation: CCPA's non-discrimination provision (1798.125) prohibits discrimination but allows financial incentives if reasonably related to data value.
OptionB: No, unless the price difference is directly related to the value of the consumer's data
OptionC: No, never under any circumstances

Title: What is the minimum age at which a minor can opt-in to the sale of their personal information under CCPA?
ContextText: Your mobile app has users under 18 in California.
Explanation: Under CCPA, minors 13-15 must opt-in themselves; for under 13, a parent/guardian must opt-in. Over 16 follows standard adult rules.
OptionB: 16 years old
OptionC: 18 years old

Title: A consumer exercises their 'Right to Delete' under CCPA. Which exception allows you to retain data?
ContextText: A customer wants all data deleted, but you have an open fraud investigation on their account.
Explanation: CCPA 1798.105(d) lists exceptions to deletion including detecting security incidents, protecting against fraud, and legal compliance.
OptionB: Detecting security incidents or fraud
OptionC: Future product development

Title: Under CCPA, what notice must be displayed when collecting personal information?
ContextText: You are designing a new checkout page for California customers.
Explanation: CCPA 1798.100(b) requires a 'Notice at Collection' that lists the categories of personal information and the purposes for collection.
OptionB: A 'Notice at Collection' listing categories and purposes
OptionC: A link to terms of service
OptionD: No notice required at point of collection

Title: How long does a business have to respond to a CCPA verifiable consumer request?
ContextText: Your team just received a consumer data access request.
Explanation: Businesses must respond within 45 calendar days. A 45-day extension is available with consumer notification for complex requests.
OptionB: 45 calendar days, extendable by 45 more
OptionC: 30 calendar days

Title: Your website must include which link for CCPA compliance?
ContextText: Legal is auditing your website for California compliance requirements.
Explanation: CCPA 1798.135 requires a clear 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link on the homepage.
OptionB: 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link
OptionC: 'Cookie Preferences' popup

Title: Under FERPA, who has the right to access a student's education records?
ContextText: A parent calls requesting their 17-year-old's high school grades.
Explanation: FERPA rights belong to parents until the student turns 18 or attends a postsecondary institution, when rights transfer to the student.
OptionB: Parents, until the student turns 18 or enters postsecondary education
OptionC: Any family member

Title: Which of the following is considered 'directory information' under FERPA?
ContextText: The athletics department wants to publish a student athlete roster.
Explanation: Directory information includes name, address, phone, dates of attendance, and participation in activities. Schools must give opt-out notice before disclosing.
OptionB: Student name and participation in activities
OptionC: Student Social Security Number

Title: A professor wants to post exam grades by student ID number on the classroom door. Is this allowed under FERPA?
ContextText: End of semester, and the professor wants a quick way to share grades.
Explanation: Student ID numbers are PII under FERPA. Posting grades with any identifiable information violates student privacy protections.
OptionA: Yes — student IDs aren't personal information
OptionB: No — student ID numbers are personally identifiable information under FERPA
OptionC: Yes, if the class is small
OptionD: Yes, if students consented at enrollment

Title: A law enforcement agency requests student records without a subpoena. What should the school do?
ContextText: Local police are investigating a crime and want access to a student's disciplinary file.
Explanation: FERPA generally requires a subpoena or court order for law enforcement access. The school must make reasonable effort to notify the student.
OptionB: Require a court order or subpoena, and notify the student unless legally prohibited
OptionC: Refuse under all circumstances

Title: A university wants to share student data with a research organization. Under FERPA, what is required?
ContextText: A research group studying dropout rates wants access to enrollment and grade data.
Explanation: FERPA's research exception requires a formal agreement covering purpose limitation, data security, and destruction requirements.
OptionA: No restrictions on sharing for research
OptionB: A written agreement limiting use, requiring data destruction after research, and prohibiting re-identification
OptionC: Student consent for each record

Title: After a student turns 18, their parent requests to see the student's grades without the student's consent. What happens?
ContextText: A concerned parent of a 19-year-old college student calls the registrar.
Explanation: At 18, FERPA rights transfer to the student. Parents can only access records with student consent or if the student is their tax dependent.
OptionA: The school must comply — parents always have access
OptionB: The school must deny unless the student provides written consent or is a tax dependent
OptionC: The school can share grades but not disciplinary records
OptionD: The school can share only if the student is failing

Title: tailgating
ContextText: Person entering behind an employee without badging in
Explanation: Tailgating is when an unauthorized person follows an authorized person through a secure entrance. Always ensure doors close between entries.

Title: unattendedBag
ContextText: Unattended backpack left near the reception desk
Explanation: Unattended bags in lobbies pose both security and safety risks. Report immediately and do not touch.

Title: photography
ContextText: Visitor taking photos of the security badge reader system
Explanation: Photographing security infrastructure could indicate reconnaissance for a social engineering or physical attack.

Title: socialEngineering
ContextText: Person in delivery uniform asking receptionist to hold the door to the secure area
Explanation: Social engineering often involves impersonating delivery personnel or contractors to gain physical access.

Title: unauthorizedAccess
ContextText: Person without a visible badge accessing server racks
Explanation: Server rooms require visible badge access. Anyone without proper identification must be challenged and reported.

Title: usbDevice
ContextText: Someone inserting a USB device into a server
Explanation: Unauthorized USB devices in server rooms can introduce malware or exfiltrate data. Only authorized maintenance should use removable media.

Title: photography
ContextText: Individual photographing server rack labels and cable configurations
Explanation: Photographing server infrastructure reveals network topology and hardware details useful for planning cyberattacks.

Title: propping
ContextText: Fire exit door propped open with a wedge
Explanation: Propped doors bypass physical access controls. Fire exits should only be used for emergencies and always self-close.

Title: shoulderSurfing
ContextText: Person standing behind a colleague viewing their screen while typing a password
Explanation: Shoulder surfing is a low-tech but effective way to steal credentials. Use privacy screens and be aware of your surroundings.

Title: cleanDesk
ContextText: Desk left with confidential documents visible and workstation unlocked overnight
Explanation: Clean desk policies prevent unauthorized viewing of sensitive information. Always lock your workstation and store documents when leaving.

Title: unauthorizedAccess
ContextText: Unknown individual walking through office without a visitor badge
Explanation: All visitors must wear visible badges. Challenge anyone without proper identification politely but firmly.

Title: dataExfiltration
ContextText: Employee photographing whiteboard with project roadmap and customer names
Explanation: Photographing confidential information on whiteboards is a form of data exfiltration. Erase boards after meetings.

Title: eavesdropping
ContextText: Person lingering outside conference room during a confidential meeting
Explanation: Confidential meetings should use rooms with sound insulation. Be aware of people lingering near meeting spaces.

Title: recordingDevice
ContextText: Small recording device spotted under the conference table
Explanation: Bug sweeps should be conducted regularly in rooms used for sensitive discussions. Report any suspicious devices immediately.

Title: unattendedLaptop
ContextText: Laptop left open and unlocked in empty conference room
Explanation: Unattended unlocked devices are easy targets for data theft or malware installation. Always lock or take your device with you.
